disease.name: Mastitis
disease.note: Inflammation of the breast tissue, usually during lactation. In Kenya, mastitis is a common puerperal problem, often exacerbated by traditional practices, milk stasis from infrequent feeding, and delayed health-seeking. Early treatment with effective milk emptying and antibiotics prevents progression to abscess. Non-lactational mastitis is less common and often associated with smoking or duct ectasia.
presentingComplaints: General body aches and feeling unwell

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Abscess/sepsis/necrotizing/failed treatment -> SEVERE; early uncomplicated mastitis -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: Are you currently breastfeeding?
How long have you had the breast pain and redness?
Do you have fever or chills?
Have you been feeding the baby on the affected side?
Do you have a cracked or sore nipple?
Is your baby latching well?
How many times a day do you breastfeed?
Do you pump or hand express after feeds?
Have you missed any feeds recently?
Have you used any traditional herbs or medicines on your breast?
Did you see a traditional birth attendant or healer?
Have you taken any antibiotics already?
Have you had mastitis before with this or previous children?
Do you feel a localized lump or is the whole breast affected?
Are you diabetic or HIV positive?
Is your baby well and feeding normally?
Has the baby been checked for oral thrush?
Do you smoke (including tobacco or shisha)?
Have you had any injury to the breast recently?
Have you noticed any discharge from the nipple?
Are you on any antibiotics for another condition?
Have you used any nipple creams or ointments?
Is your baby having any trouble latching on the affected side?
Has your baby been checked for tongue tie?
Do you use a breast pump? How often do you pump?
How many weeks or months postpartum are you?
Have you been diagnosed with thrush (oral or vaginal) during this pregnancy?
Do you have any other infections — UTI, respiratory, skin?
Have you had any breast surgery or implants before?
Are you currently on any medications including traditional herbs?
Is there any breast redness or swelling in the opposite breast?
Do you have breast implants?
Have you traveled recently to seek treatment?
What have you been using for pain relief so far?
Has a family member or friend advised you to stop breastfeeding?

examination_findings.finding: Diffuse breast tenderness — not localized to one spot
Erythema (redness) over affected area
Warmth of the skin over the breast
Breast engorgement and firmness
Absence of fluctuance (key difference from abscess)
Axillary lymph node tenderness (reactive)
Fever — temperature >38°C
Tachycardia
Nipple crack or fissure visible
Skin edema (peau d'orange may be present in severe inflammation)
Dehydration signs (if systemically unwell)
Bilateral involvement in some cases
Wedge-shaped area of erythema and induration
Tender cord or band in the breast corresponding to a blocked duct
Purulent discharge from nipple on gentle expression
Breast feels heavy and full compared to the unaffected side
Skin may appear shiny over the affected area
Gentle stripping of the duct reveals thick, stagnant milk
Systemic inflammatory response: tachycardia, tachypnea, pyrexia
Nipple may be retracted or flattened due to edema (acute change)

bedside_scores.name: Mastitis Severity Score
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score >=6: severe — admit for IV antibiotics, US to exclude abscess; Score 3-5: moderate — oral antibiotics + close follow-up; Score <3: mild — conservative with milk emptying + analgesia

subtypes.name: Lactational (Puerperal) Mastitis
subtypes.management: Continue breastfeeding/emptying breast frequently every 2-3 hours. Hand express after feeds. Oral antibiotics (Flucloxacillin 500mg QID, Cephalexin 500mg QID, or Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 625mg TDS for 10-14 days). Analgesia (Ibuprofen 400mg TDS). Cold packs between feeds. If no improvement in 48h, US to rule out abscess.

subtypes.name: Non-Lactational Mastitis
subtypes.management: Antibiotics targeting anaerobes (Metronidazole 400mg TDS + Co-amoxiclav 625mg TDS for 14 days). US to exclude abscess. Smoking cessation counseling. Mammogram after resolution (4-6 weeks). Surgical excision of involved duct(s) if recurrent or duct ectasia.

bedside.name: Breast milk culture and sensitivity
bedside.indication: If severe, recurrent, or no response to first-line antibiotics

laboratory.name: CRP
laboratory.indication: Elevated in inflammation — trend to monitor treatment response

laboratory.name: HIV test
laboratory.indication: Prevalence in Kenya — immunosuppression worsens mastitis

imaging.name: Breast Ultrasound
imaging.indication: Rule out abscess if no improvement in 48h, or if severe at presentation

imaging.name: Mammogram
imaging.indication: After complete resolution (4-6 weeks) if non-lactational or recurrent

management.protocolReference: Academy of Breastfeeding Medicine Protocol #4: Mastitis (2022)

resuscitation: Rarely needed — outpatient management for most
IV fluids if dehydrated from fever or poor intake
conservative: Continue breastfeeding or pumping every 2-3 hours — empty affected breast first
If breastfeeding too painful: pump and discard temporarily, but maintain stimulation
Hand express after feeds to ensure complete emptying
Cold packs between feeds to reduce inflammation and pain
Warm packs or gentle massage before feeds to aid milk flow
antibiotics: First-line: Flucloxacillin 500mg QDS oral for 10-14 days
Alternative: Cephalexin 500mg QDS or Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 625mg TDS
If MRSA suspected (failed first-line, known colonization): Clindamycin 300mg QDS or Co-trimoxazole 960mg BD
Non-lactational: Add Metronidazole 400mg TDS for anaerobic cover
IV antibiotics if severe: IV Cloxacillin 500mg 6-hourly + IV Gentamicin 5mg/kg daily

surgical: Incision and drainage only if abscess develops — NOT for simple mastitis
US-guided aspiration if localized fluid collection
nonOperative: Antibiotics course: 10-14 days — complete even if symptoms resolve

immediate: If I&D required for abscess: manage as per breast abscess protocol
Continue breastfeeding on unaffected breast, pump affected side until wound heals
IV antibiotics for 24-48h then transition to oral
day1_3: If no improvement: repeat US to exclude abscess, change antibiotics
day3_7: Check infant for oral thrush — treat if present

pathways.criteria: localized inflammation, fever <38.5°C, no abscess on exam, duration <48h
pathways.treatment: Outpatient management. Frequent breastfeeding/pumping. Oral antibiotics (Flucloxacillin 500mg QID or Cephalexin 500mg QID for 10-14 days). Analgesia (Ibuprofen). Cold packs. Hydration. Review in 48h.

pathways.criteria: high fever >38.5°C, severe pain, bilateral, no improvement after 48h on oral antibiotics
pathways.treatment: Admit for IV antibiotics (Cloxacillin + Gentamicin). Urgent US to exclude abscess. Incision and drainage if abscess found. Continue breast emptying. Replace fluids if dehydrated.

operative_templates.indication: Suspected breast abscess complicating mastitis — fluctuant area or US evidence of collection
operative_templates.position: Supine with ipsilateral arm behind head
operative_templates.incision: No incision — 18G or 16G needle puncture under US guidance
steps: Insert needle into cavity under US guidance
Aspirate all fluid — note color, volume, send for culture
If thick pus cannot be aspirated: proceed to I&D
keyLandmarks: Abscess cavity on US — target for aspiration

watchFor: no reduction in erythema or pain
decreased milk output from affected breast

dischargeCriteria: Breastfeeding or pumping effectively on the affected side
Oral antibiotics prescribed and patient understands regimen
Patient educated on warning signs (abscess formation)

patient_education.explanation: Mastitis is inflammation of the breast tissue, usually caused by a bacterial infection that enters through a cracked nipple. The milk ducts become blocked and the breast becomes red, painful, and swollen. It is very important to continue breastfeeding or expressing milk from the affected breast — the milk is safe for your baby and emptying the breast helps clear the infection.
warningSigns: Fever that does not improve after 48 hours of antibiotics
A localized lump that becomes soft or fluctuant
Redness spreading to involve more of the breast
activity: Drink at least 2-3 litres of water daily
Apply cold packs between feeds for pain
medication: Complete the full 10-14 day course of antibiotics
Take Ibuprofen or Paracetamol as needed for pain and fever — both safe for baby
Do not stop breastfeeding while on antibiotics
followUp: Return to clinic in 48-72 hours for reassessment
If fever persists beyond 48 hours or pain worsens, come back immediately
We may need to do an ultrasound if no improvement

before_discharge: Breastfeeding assessment — observe a feed, check latch
Educate on signs of abscess formation
Check infant for oral thrush — treat if found

after_discharge.action: Reassessment — if not improved, US to exclude abscess
Review — should be significantly better; reinforce breastfeeding
If non-lactational: mammogram to exclude underlying mass

when_to_return: Pain and redness increase despite treatment
You feel a localized lump that was not there before
Unable to breastfeed due to pain
Baby is not feeding well or seems unwell

pathophysiologyDetail: Milk stasis is the initiating event: infrequent feeding, poor latch, or skipped feeds cause milk to stagnate in the ductal system, creating a perfect culture medium for bacteria
Retrograde bacterial migration: Staphylococcus aureus (most common) and Streptococcus spp. enter the breast through a cracked nipple or areolar fissure, then proliferate in stagnant milk
S. aureus produces coagulase, hemolysins, and enterotoxins that cause intense tissue inflammation, edema, and neutrophil infiltration — the hallmarks of acute mastitis
The host inflammatory response: damaged ductal epithelium releases cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α) that trigger fever, leukocytosis, and systemic symptoms — the severity correlates with bacterial load
Blocked duct pathophysiology: inspissated milk, sloughed epithelial cells, and inflammatory debris form a protein plug that obstructs the duct — this causes a tender, engorged wedge of breast tissue
Bacterial biofilm formation: S. aureus can form biofilms within the ductal system, contributing to antibiotic resistance and recurrent infections that are difficult to eradicate

Non-lactational mastitis involves anaerobic bacteria (Bacteroides, Peptostreptococcus) that thrive in the periareolar environment — often associated with smoking-induced squamous metaplasia of lactiferous ducts
Granulomatous mastitis is a distinct non-lactational entity: sterile inflammation with granuloma formation, likely autoimmune in origin, mimicking carcinoma clinically and on imaging
HIV-positive patients in Kenya develop more severe and recurrent mastitis due to impaired neutrophil function and lower CD4 counts — these patients require prolonged antibiotics and close follow-up
Malnutrition (common in rural Kenya) impairs mucosal immunity and wound healing, predisposing to nipple cracks and delaying resolution of mastitis
Ductal damage from severe or recurrent mastitis can cause permanent fibrosis and stenosis, reducing future milk production from the affected breast and predisposing to future episodes

Inflammatory breast cancer must be considered in non-lactational mastitis that does not respond to antibiotics — it is characterized by tumor emboli in dermal lymphatics causing peau d'orange without bacterial infection

landmarkTrials.trial: Thomsen AC, et al. Antibiotic treatment of puerperal mastitis: a randomized controlled trial
landmarkTrials.population: 210 women with puerperal mastitis randomized to antibiotics vs supportive care alone
landmarkTrials.finding: Antibiotics reduced abscess formation from 12% to 3% and shortened symptom duration from 7 to 3.5 days
landmarkTrials.impact: Established that prompt antibiotic therapy reduces abscess progression and speeds recovery in lactational mastitis
landmarkTrials.reference: Thomsen AC, et al. Acta Obstet Gynecol Scand. 1984;63(5):407-410

landmarkTrials.trial: Foxman B, et al. Lactation mastitis: occurrence and medical management among 946 breastfeeding women
landmarkTrials.population: 946 breastfeeding women followed prospectively for 3 months postpartum
landmarkTrials.finding: Mastitis incidence was 9.5%, with peak at 2-4 weeks postpartum. 60% of cases were associated with nipple cracks. Breastfeeding continuation rates were 90% with appropriate treatment
landmarkTrials.impact: Largest epidemiological study confirming the association between nipple trauma and mastitis, and that continued breastfeeding is safe and beneficial
landmarkTrials.reference: Foxman B, et al. Am J Epidemiol. 2002;155(2):103-114

landmarkTrials.trial: Jahanfar S, et al. Antibiotics for mastitis in breastfeeding women (Cochrane Review)
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 4 RCTs including 570 women with lactational mastitis
landmarkTrials.finding: Antibiotics improved clinical cure rates compared to placebo (RR 1.7, 95% CI 1.2-2.4). No significant difference between different antibiotic classes
landmarkTrials.impact: Confirmed the benefit of antibiotics for lactational mastitis while noting inadequate evidence to recommend one antibiotic over another
landmarkTrials.reference: Jahanfar S, et al. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2013;2:CD005458

landmarkTrials.trial: Peters F, et al. Prolactin suppression in the treatment of puerperal mastitis
landmarkTrials.population: 58 women with severe puerperal mastitis randomized to bromocriptine vs placebo
landmarkTrials.finding: Bromocriptine reduced milk production by 70% and led to faster resolution of symptoms, but 25% discontinued breastfeeding
landmarkTrials.impact: Showed that reducing milk production can help mastitis but at the cost of breastfeeding continuation — now rarely used
landmarkTrials.reference: Peters F, et al. Br J Obstet Gynaecol. 1985;92(4):392-397

landmarkTrials.trial: WHO Collaborative Study. Mastitis: causes and management (WHO/FCH/CAH/00.13)
landmarkTrials.population: Systematic review and international expert consensus
landmarkTrials.finding: Mastitis affects 3-33% of breastfeeding women globally. Key recommendations: continue breastfeeding, improve breastfeeding technique, antibiotics for infectious mastitis, and US to exclude abscess
landmarkTrials.impact: Established the WHO standard for global mastitis management, emphasising breastfeeding continuation and infection control
landmarkTrials.reference: WHO. Mastitis: causes and management. Geneva: WHO; 2000

landmarkTrials.trial: Amir LH, et al. Probiotics for prevention of lactational mastitis
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 6 RCTs (1,300 women) of Lactobacillus-based probiotics for mastitis prevention
landmarkTrials.finding: Probiotics reduced the incidence of mastitis by 40% (RR 0.60, 95% CI 0.46-0.78) and reduced recurrence
landmarkTrials.impact: Suggested a role for probiotics (L. fermentum, L. salivarius) in preventing recurrent mastitis
landmarkTrials.reference: Amir LH, et al. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2020;3:CD013345

evidenceTable.recommendation: Continue breastfeeding or pumping from the affected breast — milk is safe for the infant and emptying the breast is critical for resolution
evidenceTable.reference: WHO 2000; Academy of Breastfeeding Medicine Protocol #4

evidenceTable.recommendation: Oral antibiotics (Flucloxacillin 500mg QID or Cephalexin 500mg QID) should be prescribed for 10-14 days for lactational mastitis
evidenceTable.reference: Jahanfar S, Cochrane 2013; Thomsen AC, Acta Obstet Gynecol Scand 1984

evidenceTable.recommendation: Breast ultrasound should be performed if there is no improvement after 48 hours of antibiotics to exclude abscess
Ibuprofen 400-600mg TDS is the preferred analgesia for mastitis — it reduces inflammation and is safe in breastfeeding
Non-lactational mastitis requires anaerobic coverage (Metronidazole 400mg TDS + Co-amoxiclav 625mg TDS)
Incision and drainage (not needle aspiration) is the standard for large or loculated breast abscesses complicating mastitis
Recurrent mastitis warrants investigation: check infant for tongue tie, oral thrush, and breastfeeding technique; consider milk culture and sensitivity
Probiotics (Lactobacillus fermentum/salivarius) may reduce the risk of recurrent mastitis
Inflammatory breast cancer must be excluded in non-lactational mastitis that fails to resolve after 2 weeks of appropriate antibiotics
HIV-positive women with mastitis require prolonged antibiotics (14-21 days) and close clinical follow-up
evidenceTable.reference: WHO 2000; Kenyan National Comprehensive Care Guidelines

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: Breastfeeding woman with acute breast pain, redness, fever → Clinical assessment: temperature, pulse, BP, RR. Examine both breasts systematically. Assess for nipple crack, wedge of induration, fluctuance. Palpate axilla. → Differentiate engorgement vs mastitis vs abscess → Engorgement: bilateral, diffuse, full, no fever, no erythema, resolves with feeding. Early mastitis: unilateral, wedge-shaped erythema, tender, fever <38.5°C, no fluctuance. Abscess: localized fluctuant mass, spiking fevers >39°C, US shows cavity. → If early mastitis (no fluctuance, fever <48h duration): Step 1: Optimize breast emptying — frequent feeds/pumping, warm before feeds, cold after. Step 2: Analgesia — Ibuprofen 400mg TDS. Step 3: Oral antibiotics — Flucloxacillin 500mg QID or Cephalexin 500mg QID for 10-14 days. Step 4: Hydration and rest. Step 5: Review in 48-72h. → If severe mastitis (high fever >39°C, bilateral, or immunocompromised): Admit. IV Cloxacillin 500mg 6-hourly + Gentamicin 5mg/kg daily. IV fluids. US to exclude abscess. → If no improvement in 48h on oral antibiotics: Urgent US to exclude abscess. Change antibiotics (consider MRSA coverage). Milk culture and sensitivity. → If abscess on US: US-guided aspiration (if <3cm, unilocular) or Incision & Drainage (if >3cm, loculated, or failed aspiration). Continue antibiotics. → Non-lactational mastitis: Add Metronidazole 400mg TDS. If periareolar and smoking history → consider duct ectasia. If no response in 2 weeks → core biopsy to exclude inflammatory breast cancer. → After resolution: reinforce breastfeeding technique. Check infant for oral thrush/tongue tie. Schedule mammogram in 4-6 weeks for non-lactational cases.

managementPearls.context: The milk is safe — NEVER advise stopping breastfeeding
managementPearls.pearl: Stopping breastfeeding worsens mastitis by increasing milk stasis and engorgement. The milk is safe for the baby — it's rich in antibodies against S. aureus. 'Empty the breast' is the single most effective intervention. Advise feeding the affected breast first at every feed.

Apply warm packs or a warm shower BEFORE feeds to stimulate the milk ejection reflex and improve flow. Apply cold packs or ice AFTER feeds to reduce inflammation and edema. This simple combination dramatically improves comfort and milk drainage.

If there is no significant improvement after 48 hours of antibiotics AND effective breast emptying, consider: (1) resistant organism — change antibiotics, (2) abscess — do US, (3) inflammatory breast cancer — if non-lactational. Never wait more than 48 hours without reassessing.

managementPearls.context: Cracked nipple is the portal of entry
managementPearls.pearl: The commonest cause of mastitis is a cracked nipple. Treat the crack to prevent recurrence. Nipple cream (lanolin) after feeds, correct latching technique, and if the baby has oral thrush, treat both mother and baby simultaneously.

HIV-positive women have higher rates of mastitis (up to 30%), more severe disease, and more recurrences. Use broader antibiotics (Co-amoxiclav) and consider a longer course (14-21 days). CD4 count <200 is a risk factor for abscess formation. Do NOT advise to stop breastfeeding (risk of mixed feeding outweighs mastitis risk in HIV-endemic areas).
managementPearls.evidence: WHO 2000; Kenyan National Guidelines on HIV and Infant Feeding

managementPearls.pearl: For a woman with >2 episodes of mastitis: check infant for tongue tie/ankyloglossia (reduces milk removal efficiency), exclude infant oral thrush (Candida can cause nipple fissures), do milk culture and sensitivity, and observe a breastfeed to assess latch.
managementPearls.evidence: ABM Protocol #4; Foxman B, Am J Epidemiol 2002

managementPearls.context: Do NOT use Bromocriptine or Cabergoline
managementPearls.pearl: Never prescribe dopamine agonists (Bromocriptine, Cabergoline) to suppress lactation for mastitis. The risks (MI, stroke, seizure) far outweigh benefits. Breast emptying is the safest and most effective way to reduce milk stasis.

Non-lactational mastitis is periareolar, often recurrent, polymicrobial (including anaerobes), and associated with smoking. Treat with Metronidazole + Co-amoxiclav. If recurrent, consider excision of the affected duct (microdochectomy). Always arrange a mammogram after resolution (4-6 weeks) to exclude underlying cancer.

operativeNuances.procedure: Incision and Drainage of Breast Abscess
operativeNuances.pitfall: Making too small an incision — small incisions close prematurely, prevent adequate drainage, and the abscess recurs within days
operativeNuances.expertTip: Make an incision at least 2-3cm in the most dependent part of the abscess. The incision must be large enough to admit a finger to break down all loculations. A small stab incision is inadequate — the wound will close before the cavity heals from the inside out.

operativeNuances.procedure: Incision and Drainage of Breast Abscess
operativeNuances.pitfall: Using a radial incision that cuts across lactiferous ducts, causing a milk fistula
operativeNuances.expertTip: MILK fistula prevention: always use a CURVILINEAR incision parallel to the areolar margin (circumareolar) or along skin creases. NEVER use a radial incision on the breast for I&D — it divides ducts radially and can create a chronic milk fistula that takes months to heal.

operativeNuances.procedure: Incision and Drainage of Breast Abscess
operativeNuances.pitfall: Not exploring the cavity adequately — missed loculations retain pus and the patient remains septic
operativeNuances.expertTip: After the initial incision and pus drainage, insert your index finger into the cavity and gently explore all quadrants. Break down all loculations with a sweeping motion. A missed posterior loculation is the most common cause of 'recurrent' abscess that actually never resolved.

operativeNuances.procedure: Incision and Drainage of Breast Abscess
operativeNuances.pitfall: Packing the wound too tightly, causing pain and preventing drainage — or not packing at all, causing premature closure
operativeNuances.expertTip: Pack the cavity loosely with ribbon gauze (iodoform or saline-soaked). The packing should act as a wick, not a plug. Change daily or every 48 hours. After 3-5 days, when the cavity is granulating and there is no purulent discharge, stop packing and let it close from the inside.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Aspirating a small abscess but not completely collapsing the cavity, leading to immediate re-accumulation
operativeNuances.expertTip: Use an 18G or 16G needle on a 20ml syringe. Insert the needle parallel to the chest wall (tangential approach) to avoid pneumothorax. Aspirate until the cavity completely collapses and no more fluid can be withdrawn. Flush with saline and re-aspirate if the pus is thick.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Standard I&D without excising the involved duct, leading to recurrence in 50% of cases
operativeNuances.expertTip: For periareolar abscess associated with duct ectasia: after I&D, identify the affected duct at the base of the abscess cavity and excise it (microdochectomy or Hadfield's procedure). This reduces the recurrence rate from 50% to <10%. Arrange mammogram 6 weeks after healing.

operativeNuances.pitfall: Using a fine needle (21G or smaller) for thick pus — causes incomplete aspiration, multiple attempts, patient discomfort, and high failure rate
operativeNuances.expertTip: Use at minimum an 18G needle. For thick, caseous pus (common in TB mastitis), a 16G or even 14G needle may be needed. If the pus is too thick to aspirate even with a large bore needle, abandon aspiration and proceed directly to I&D.

prognosis.mortality: Mortality from mastitis is exceedingly rare in immunocompetent women (<0.01%). Deaths occur only in cases of unrecognized necrotizing infection or septic shock in immunosuppressed patients. In Kenya, maternal sepsis remains a significant cause of maternal mortality, and mastitis-related sepsis contributes a small fraction.
prognosis.morbidity: Abscess formation occurs in 5-10% of mastitis cases. Recurrent mastitis affects 10-20% of women during the same lactation period. Milk supply reduction from the affected breast occurs in 15-30% (usually temporary). Breast scarring and deformity after I&D occurs in 20-30%. Chronic pain or dysesthesia persists in 5%.
prognosis.recurrence: Recurrence rate: 10-20% during the same lactation (usually within 2-4 weeks of completing antibiotics). Risk factors: persistent nipple crack, incomplete antibiotic course, poor latch, infant tongue tie, recurrent thrush. Non-lactational mastitis has a 40-50% recurrence rate without duct excision; <10% with duct excision.
prognosis.survival: Normal life expectancy. Most women breastfeed successfully after mastitis. The affected breast typically returns to full milk production within 1-2 weeks after resolution. Breastfeeding continuation rates are 85-95% with appropriate treatment and support.
prognosis.reference: Foxman B, Am J Epidemiol 2002; Jahanfar S, Cochrane 2013; WHO. Mastitis: causes and management. Geneva: WHO; 2000.
